title: Market Structures
subject: Economics
objectives: Identify the four main market structures

sections.heading: The four market structures
sections.content:
Economists classify markets into four structures based on the number of firms, the type of product, and barriers to entry:

1. Perfect competition: Many small firms sell identical products. No single firm can influence the price. Examples: agricultural commodities (wheat, corn).

2. Monopolistic competition: Many firms sell similar but differentiated products. Each firm has a small degree of pricing power. Examples: restaurants, clothing brands.

3. Oligopoly: A few large firms dominate the market. Their decisions are interdependent. Examples: airlines, smartphone manufacturers, car companies.

4. Monopoly: A single firm is the sole seller. It has significant pricing power. Examples: local utility companies, patented drugs.

sections.heading: Comparing market structures
sections.content:
Feature comparison:

• Number of firms: Perfect competition (many) → Monopoly (one)
• Product type: Identical → Differentiated → Differentiated → Unique
• Price control: None → Some → Considerable → Significant
• Barriers to entry: None → Low → High → Very high
• Examples: Corn farmers → Coffee shops → Boeing/Airbus → Your local water company

As we move from perfect competition toward monopoly, firms gain more market power and consumers have fewer choices.

Market structure determines how much power firms have over prices. Perfect competition gives firms zero pricing power; monopolies have the most. Most real-world markets fall somewhere between these extremes.

aiContext: This lesson covers market structures at a high school microeconomics level. Students learn about perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly, comparing their characteristics and real-world examples.
